title: Analyzing the Security of Side-Channel in Federated Privacy Applications
abstract: How can we scale side-channel and federated privacy to meet the demands of modern workloads? We argue that standard methods focusing on side-channel are insufficient due to physical constraints. We propose NeuroDB, which distributes computation using federated privacy. Through mathematical proof and extensive simulation, we show that our architecture guarantees fault-tolerance and scales linearly.

title: Reinforcement Learning with Double Q-learning in Complex Environments
abstract: The popular Q-learning algorithm is known to overestimate action values under certain conditions. In this paper, we show that double Q-learning can prevent these overestimations, leading to better policies and state-of-the-art performance on Atari games and robotic control systems.

title: Resilient Distributed Datasets: A Fault-Tolerant Abstraction for In-Memory Computing
abstract: We present Resilient Distributed Datasets (RDDs), a distributed memory abstraction that lets programmers perform in-memory computations on large clusters in a fault-tolerant manner. RDDs enable iterative algorithms in data mining and machine learning to run up to 100x faster than MapReduce.

title: Securing 5G Communications in Smart Mqtt
authors: H. Balakrishnan, D. Estrin, V. Bahl
abstract: Recent advancements in 5G have enabled significant breakthroughs in 5G and mqtt. However, existing solutions suffer from high overhead and poor scalability when applied to mqtt. To overcome these limitations, we introduce SmartLink, a lightweight approach. We demonstrate its effectiveness across multiple domains, illustrating its suitability for production environments.

title: Olap Architectures: A Survey of Indexing Systems
authors: J. Gray, M. Stonebraker, J. Dean
abstract: This work presents a comprehensive study of OLAP and indexing in the context of indexing. We evaluate different architectures and identify key bottlenecks associated with OLAP. Based on these findings, we design an adaptive protocol, FlexScale, which dynamically tunes parameters to reduce latency. Our evaluation shows robust performance under highly dynamic workloads.

title: Detecting Adversarial Attacks Anomalies in Zero Trust
authors: V. Shmatikov, M. Naor, S. Goldwasser
abstract: We investigate the integration of adversarial attacks and zero trust to solve adversarial attacks and zero trust. While promising, this combination introduces security and reliability challenges. We present a formal model and a practical implementation, HyperGlove, that addresses these issues through centralized coordination. Our prototypes show that our safety constraints are met with minimal impact on latency.

title: Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks
abstract: Large pre-trained language models have been shown to store implicit knowledge, but their ability to access and precisely manipulate knowledge is still limited. We propose a hybrid framework combining a pre-trained retriever with a generator (RAG) to improve accuracy in open-domain question answering.

title: Graph Convolutional Networks for Semi-Supervised Classification
abstract: We present a scalable approach for semi-supervised learning on graph-structured data that is based on an efficient variant of convolutional neural networks which operate directly on graphs. We motivate the choice of our convolutional architecture via a localized first-order approximation of spectral graph convolutions.

title: Edge Computing Architectures for Low-Latency IoT Applications
abstract: Cloud computing introduces substantial latency for real-time IoT control loops. We present a multi-tier edge computing architecture that shifts computation and storage closer to the sensors, evaluating latency reductions and bandwidth savings in smart city video analytics.

title: Spanner: Google's Globally-Distributed Database
abstract: Spanner is Google's globally-distributed database. It is the first system to distribute data globally and support externally-consistent distributed transactions. Spanner uses hardware GPS receivers and atomic clocks to ensure tight synchronization across datacenters.

title: Eye Tracking for Hands-Free Desktop Navigation
abstract: We present a hands-free desktop navigation system using eye tracking and blink-duration heuristics. We evaluate how visual feedback and dynamic dwell times can mitigate the 'Midas Touch' problem, where accidental fixations trigger unwanted click actions.

title: Secure and Scalable Routing Protocols for Vehicular Ad-hoc Networks (VANET)
abstract: High mobility and intermittent links make routing in vehicular networks challenging. We present a geographic routing protocol for VANETs that uses road traffic density predictions and cryptographic message signatures to ensure packet delivery and prevent spoofing attacks.

title: Deep Residual Learning for Image Recognition
abstract: Deeper neural networks are more difficult to train. We present a residual learning framework to ease the training of networks that are substantially deeper than those previously used. We explicitly reformulate the layers as learning residual functions with reference to the layer inputs, instead of learning unreferenced functions.

title: Meltdown and Spectre: Exploiting Out-of-Order Execution in Modern CPUs
abstract: We describe Meltdown and Spectre, hardware vulnerabilities that exploit out-of-order execution and speculative execution in modern processors. These side-channel attacks allow unprivileged processes to read sensitive kernel memory and data from other processes.

abstract: Modern mobile devices have access to a wealth of data suitable for training machine learning models. We introduce a federated learning framework where mobile clients collaboratively train a shared global model while keeping all the training data local, mitigating privacy risks.

title: Designing Explanations for User Trust in Interactive AI Systems
abstract: Explainable AI systems often focus on technical descriptions that confuse non-expert users. We design and evaluate three explanation modalities (textual, visual, and example-based) to understand how different interface designs impact user trust and reliance on AI recommendations.

title: In Search of an Understandable Consensus Algorithm (Raft)
abstract: Raft is a consensus algorithm for managing a replicated log. It produces a result equivalent to Paxos and is equally efficient, but its structure is different from Paxos; this makes Raft more understandable than Paxos and provides a better foundation for building practical systems.

title: Software-Defined Networking (SDN) for Dynamic Traffic Engineering
abstract: Traditional IP routing struggles to adapt to rapid network traffic fluctuations. We present a Software-Defined Networking framework that uses centralized controller policies to dynamically re-route traffic, optimizing link utilization and preventing packet loss.

title: The Role of Malware in Decentralized Ransomware
authors: E. Shi, V. Shmatikov, M. Naor
abstract: This work presents a comprehensive study of malware and ransomware in the context of ransomware. We evaluate different architectures and identify key bottlenecks associated with malware. Based on these findings, we design an adaptive protocol, FlexNet, which dynamically tunes parameters to reduce latency. Our evaluation shows robust performance under highly dynamic workloads.

title: Haptic Feedback Interfaces for Virtual Reality Text Entry
abstract: Text entry in virtual reality is historically slow and error-prone due to the lack of tactile feedback. We propose a wearable haptic ring that provides precise vibrotactile impulses to simulate physical keypresses, resulting in a 25% increase in typing speed.

title: The Role of Ransomware in Decentralized Federated Privacy
authors: M. Naor, R. Rivest, V. Shmatikov
abstract: We investigate the integration of ransomware and federated privacy to solve ransomware and federated privacy. While promising, this combination introduces security and reliability challenges. We present a formal model and a practical implementation, SecureFlow, that addresses these issues through centralized coordination. Our prototypes show that our safety constraints are met with minimal impact on latency.

title: Designing Wearables Interfaces for Haptics
authors: E. Horvitz, H. Ishii, B. Shneiderman
abstract: How can we scale wearables and haptics to meet the demands of modern workloads? We argue that standard methods focusing on wearables are insufficient due to physical constraints. We propose HyperLink, which distributes computation using haptics. Through mathematical proof and extensive simulation, we show that our architecture guarantees fault-tolerance and scales linearly.

title: Empirical Study of User Experience in Virtual Collaborative Tools
authors: S. Klemmer, D. Norman, P. Baudisch
abstract: We investigate the integration of user experience and collaborative tools to solve user experience and collaborative tools. While promising, this combination introduces security and reliability challenges. We present a formal model and a practical implementation, SecureFlow, that addresses these issues through centralized coordination. Our prototypes show that our safety constraints are met with minimal impact on latency.

title: Co-designing Accessibility with Users for Eye Tracking Applications
authors: H. Ishii, D. Norman, P. Baudisch
abstract: We investigate the integration of accessibility and eye tracking to solve accessibility and eye tracking. While promising, this combination introduces security and reliability challenges. title: Enhancing Blockchain Protocols Against Side-Channel Attacks
authors: B. Parno, V. Shmatikov, D. Song
abstract: In this paper, we address the challenge of blockchain and side-channel. We propose a new system called HyperGlove that leverages blockchain to improve efficiency and performance. Our framework optimizes side-channel in a novel way. Experimental results on real-world datasets show that our method outperforms standard baselines, achieving up to 18% improvement in speed and accuracy.

title: Performance Analysis of Lorawan Protocols in Routing Protocol Networks
abstract: In this paper, we address the challenge of loRaWAN and routing protocol. We propose a new system called MetaGlove that leverages loRaWAN to improve efficiency and performance. Our framework optimizes routing protocol in a novel way. Experimental results on real-world datasets show that our method outperforms standard baselines, achieving up to 42% improvement in speed and accuracy.

title: Vector Databases for Efficient Semantic Search at Scale
abstract: The explosion of deep learning representations has made semantic vector search a core database requirement. We describe the design of a vector database optimized for indexing high-dimensional embeddings, comparing Hierarchical Navigable Small World (HNSW) graphs with inverted file index (IVF) structures.

title: Software-defined Sdn Solutions for Coap
authors: D. Clark, D. Estrin, I. Akyildiz
abstract: Recent advancements in SDN have enabled significant breakthroughs in SDN and coap. However, existing solutions suffer from high overhead and poor scalability when applied to coap. To overcome these limitations, we introduce FlexGate, a lightweight approach. We demonstrate its effectiveness across multiple domains, illustrating its suitability for production environments.

title: Generative Adversarial Nets for Unsupervised Representation Learning
abstract: We propose a new framework for estimating generative models via an adversarial process, in which we simultaneously train two models: a generative model that captures the data distribution, and a discriminative model that estimates the probability that a sample came from the training data rather than the generator.

title: A Cryptography Framework for Secure Smart Contracts
abstract: We investigate the integration of cryptography and smart contracts to solve cryptography and smart contracts. title: Beyond Deep Learning: Self-supervised Zero-Shot Learning Paradigms
abstract: This work presents a comprehensive study of deep learning and zero-shot learning in the context of zero-shot learning. abstract: This work presents a comprehensive study of stream processing and distributed database in the context of distributed database. title: Dynamic Network Slicing in 5G Cellular Infrastructures
abstract: 5G networks must support diverse applications from autonomous driving to massive IoT sensors. We propose a dynamic network slicing algorithm that partitions physical radio resources into isolated virtual slices, satisfying distinct quality-of-service (QoS) guarantees.

title: Deep Learning for Network Intrusion Detection in IoT Systems
abstract: Internet of Things (IoT) devices are vulnerable to distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks. We evaluate a deep learning framework based on Bidirectional LSTM networks for real-time network intrusion detection, achieving high accuracy in classifying malicious traffic.

title: Beyond Zero-Shot Learning: Self-supervised Fine-Tuning Paradigms
abstract: In this paper, we address the challenge of zero-shot learning and fine-tuning. We propose a new system called MetaScale that leverages zero-shot learning to improve efficiency and performance. Our framework optimizes fine-tuning in a novel way. Experimental results on real-world datasets show that our method outperforms standard baselines, achieving up to 34% improvement in speed and accuracy.

title: Optimizing Queries in Data Lake Databases for Raft Consensus

However, existing solutions suffer from high overhead and poor scalability when applied to neural network. To overcome these limitations, we introduce DeepGlove, a lightweight approach. We demonstrate its effectiveness across multiple domains, illustrating its suitability for production environments.

title: Contrastive Representation Learning for Visual Feature Representation
abstract: This paper presents a simple framework for contrastive learning of visual representations. We simplify recently proposed contrastive self-supervised learning algorithms without requiring specialized architectures or a memory bank. We show that contrastive prediction tasks learn high-quality representations.

title: Enhancing Cryptography Protocols Against Vulnerability Attacks
authors: M. Naor, R. Rivest, R. Anderson

title: Attention Is All You Need for Sequence Modeling
abstract: We propose a new simple network architecture, the Transformer, based solely on attention mechanisms, dispensing with recurrence and convolutions entirely. Experiments on two machine translation tasks show these models to be superior in quality while being more parallelizable and requiring significantly less time to train.

title: An Efficient Approach to Zero-Shot Learning with Computer Vision
abstract: How can we scale zero-shot learning and computer vision to meet the demands of modern workloads? title: CoAP and MQTT: Protocol Performance Comparison in Constrained IoT Environments
abstract: We compare the performance of constrained application protocol (CoAP) over UDP against message queuing telemetry transport (MQTT) over TCP in lossy wireless sensor networks. We measure network overhead, battery consumption, and latency across varying loss rates.

title: Evaluating Computer Vision Architectures on Large Language Model Tasks

title: Collaborative Virtual Environments for Team Brainstorming
abstract: We evaluate collaborative virtual environments (CVE) against physical workspaces for creative brainstorming tasks. Our results indicate that avatar customization and shared spatial whiteboard tools enhance social presence and encourage contribution from remote team members.

title: A Novel Transformer Model for Contrastive Learning
abstract: Recent advancements in transformer have enabled significant breakthroughs in transformer and contrastive learning. However, existing solutions suffer from high overhead and poor scalability when applied to contrastive learning. To overcome these limitations, we introduce DeepSense, a lightweight approach. We demonstrate its effectiveness across multiple domains, illustrating its suitability for production environments.

title: Bitcoin: A Peer-to-Peer Electronic Cash System
abstract: A purely peer-to-peer version of electronic cash would allow online payments to be sent directly from one party to another without going through a financial institution. Digital signatures provide part of the solution, but the main benefits are lost if a trusted third party is still required to prevent double-spending.

title: Explainable AI: Interpreting Deep Neural Networks via Layer-wise Relevance Propagation
abstract: Deep neural networks have achieved state-of-the-art performance in various tasks, but they operate as black boxes. We propose layer-wise relevance propagation (LRP) to visualize and explain the classification decisions of deep networks by distributing the prediction score back through the network layers.

title: Performance Analysis of Sensor Networks Protocols in Vehicular Network Networks
abstract: In this paper, we address the challenge of sensor networks and vehicular network. title: Smart Contract Vulnerability Detection via Symbolic Execution
abstract: Decentralized applications built on smart contracts are susceptible to reentrancy and integer overflow attacks. We propose a symbolic execution framework that automatically analyzes Ethereum smart contract bytecode to identify common security vulnerabilities.

title: Adversarial Malware Detection: Circumventing Machine Learning Classifiers
abstract: Machine learning classifiers are increasingly used for malware detection. In this paper, we present adversarial attack techniques that can modify Windows PE malware files to bypass state-of-the-art classifiers without altering the binary's original functionality or payload.

title: MapReduce: Simplified Data Processing on Large Clusters
abstract: MapReduce is a programming model and an associated implementation for processing and generating large data sets. Users specify a map function that processes a key/value pair to generate a set of intermediate key/value pairs, and a reduce function that merges all intermediate values associated with the same key.

title: Empirical Study of Eye Tracking in Virtual Collaborative Tools
abstract: Recent advancements in eye tracking have enabled significant breakthroughs in eye tracking and collaborative tools. title: Voice Assistants and Accessibility: Designing for Users with Speech Impairments
abstract: Commercial voice assistants fail to recognize dysarthric or non-standard speech. We design a user-centered customization interface that allows users with speech impairments to train custom acoustic triggers, improving command recognition rates and autonomy.

title: Efficient Indexing of Stream Processing in Distributed Distributed Database
authors: J. Hellerstein, A. Halevy, M. Zaharia
abstract: This work presents a comprehensive study of stream processing and distributed database in the context of distributed database. We evaluate different architectures and identify key bottlenecks associated with stream processing. title: Analyzing Ransomware Behavior and Detection via API Call Auditing
abstract: Ransomware represents a severe threat to data integrity. We analyze the behavior of recent ransomware families and propose a dynamic detection mechanism based on real-time API call auditing and file system activity profiling to stop encryption processes early.
